title: David’s EDA
text:
Introduction
Codelibrary(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)
library(tigris)
library(shiny)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(stringr)

source(here::here("data", "data_cleaning.R"))

## Theme Settings
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

scale_use_level <- function(...) {
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name = NULL, ...)
}

scale_use_level_fill <- function(...) {
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name = NULL, ...)
}

title: David’s EDA
text: Based on the payroll/n_emp and ai usage, how much is the revenue changing? Are those firms who are paying their employee more seeing more gains? Or is it a matter of company size?

title: David’s EDA
text:
The AI Wage Premium
Does the AI wage premium holds constant as firms grow in size?

Code## wage premium story
tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
         used_tech != "Unknown",
         naics_sector != "Total", 
         payroll > 0,
         n_emp > 0) |>
  mutate(
    ai_used = if_else(used_tech == "Used", "AI Adopters", "Non-Adopters"),
    payroll_per_emp = payroll / n_emp) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = n_emp, y = payroll_per_emp, color = ai_used)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.5) + 
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(suffix = "K"), 
    breaks = seq(0, 300, by = 50)
  ) +  
  scale_use_level() +
  labs(
    title = "Does AI Pay?",
    subtitle = "Evidence of higher payroll standards among technology adopters across firm scales",
    x = "Number of Employees (Log Scale)",
    y = "Avg. Payroll per Employee (USD)",
    color = "Adoption Status",
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios\nNote: Payroll standardized to thousands of dollars per employee. Trend lines calculated via local regression (LOESS)."
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8))

title: David’s EDA
text:
Patterns within Industries
Company Size Panel Clean (2017-2021)

While adoption alone signals a premium, intensity is the true driver. In sectors like Finance and Information, high implementation leads to the most significant wage increases.


Code# Management sector
tech_2018_clean |> 
  filter(naics_sector == "Management", tech_type == "artificial intelligence") |> 
  group_by(use_level) |> 
  summarise(firm_count = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  arrange(use_level)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  use_level                                        firm_count
  <chr>                                                 <dbl>
1 Did not use                                           16618
2 Don't know                                             2884
3 High use                                                  0
4 Low use                                                1194
5 Moderate use                                            211
6 Tested, but did not use in production or service        159

title: David’s EDA
text:
Patterns within Industries

Code# Ensure the scaling matches your data source
# If payroll is $80,000, scale = 0.001 makes it $80K
# If payroll is already 80, scale = 0.001 makes it $0.08K (incorrect)

tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    naics_sector %in% c("Information", "Finance and Insurance", 
                        "Professional Services", "Management"),
    use_level != "Don't know",
    n_emp > 0, 
    payroll > 0
  ) |>
  mutate(
    payroll_per_emp = payroll / n_emp,
    use_level = fct_relevel(use_level,
                            "Did not use",
                            "Tested, but did not use in production or service",
                            "Low use", "Moderate use", "High use")
  ) |>
  group_by(naics_sector, use_level) |> 
  summarise(
    wpay = weighted.mean(payroll_per_emp, w = n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = use_level, y = wpay, fill = use_level)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  scale_use_level_fill() +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(
    "Did not use" = "None",
    "Tested, but did not use in production or service" = "Tested",
    "Low use" = "Low", "Moderate use" = "Moderate", "High use" = "High"
  )) +
  # This line is the most common point of failure:
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar( suffix = "K")) +
  facet_wrap(~naics_sector, scales = "free_y") + 
  labs(
    title = "AI Intensity and Payroll",
    subtitle = "Higher levels of AI implementation correlate with significant wage premiums",
    x = NULL, 
    y = "Avg. Payroll per Employee",
    caption = "Source: 2018 ABS. Note: 'High Use' category for Management contains zero or suppressed reporting firms."
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

title: David’s EDA
text:
AI Adoption Spatial

Note: industry sectors with more than 10 missing states were excluded from this analysis. These sectors are Agriculture, Mining, and Utilities. The remaining sectors provide a more reliable look at AI adoption trends. AI adoption rates are rendered to two decimal places to capture variations in sectors where adoption is emerging but still below 1%.

title: David’s EDA
Isauro R
text: EDA 1: Average Number of Firms

title: Isauro R
text:
By Types of Tech

Codetech_2018_clean |>
  group_by(tech_type) |>
  summarize(avgFirms = mean(n_firms)) |>
  arrange(desc(avgFirms))

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  tech_type               avgFirms
  <chr>                      <dbl>
1 robotics                   1530.
2 specialized equipment      1516.
3 artificial intelligence    1514.
4 cloud-based                1510.
5 specialized software       1502.



Among Tech Companies in the North America, Robotics companies have the highest average number of firms at 1529.508. Specialized Software companies report the lowest at 1501.812 firms.

By Tech Usage

Codetech_2018_clean |>
  group_by(used_tech) |>
  summarize(avgFirms = mean(n_firms)) |>
  arrange(desc(avgFirms))

# A tibble: 4 × 2
  used_tech   avgFirms
  <chr>          <dbl>
1 Did not use   6872. 
2 Used           567. 
3 Unknown        485. 
4 Tested only     30.1



Among Tech Companies in the North America, Companies that Did not use their tech have the highest average number of firms at 6872.37554. Companies that Tested only their tech report the lowest at 30.13016 firms.

By Types of Tech and If They Were Used

Codetech_2018_clean |>
  group_by(tech_type, used_tech) |>
  summarize(avgFirms = mean(n_firms)) |>
  arrange(desc(avgFirms))

# A tibble: 20 × 3
# Groups:   tech_type [5]
   tech_type               used_tech   avgFirms
   <chr>                   <chr>          <dbl>
 1 robotics                Did not use   8631. 
 2 artificial intelligence Did not use   8298. 
 3 specialized equipment   Did not use   6966. 
 4 cloud-based             Did not use   5503. 
 5 specialized software    Did not use   4964. 
 6 specialized software    Used          1163. 
 7 cloud-based             Used           996. 
 8 specialized equipment   Used           531. 
 9 specialized software    Unknown        526. 
10 specialized equipment   Unknown        522. 
11 cloud-based             Unknown        521. 
12 artificial intelligence Unknown        502. 
13 robotics                Unknown        351. 
14 artificial intelligence Used            82.3
15 robotics                Used            60.2
16 cloud-based             Tested only     51.4
17 artificial intelligence Tested only     38.1
18 specialized software    Tested only     30.6
19 specialized equipment   Tested only     16.1
20 robotics                Tested only     14.5



Among Tech Companies in the North America, Robotics companies that Did not use their tech have the highest average number of firms at 8630.75257. Robotics companies that Tested only their tech report the lowest at 14.45658 firms.

title: Isauro R
text:
EDA 2: Average Payroll per Employee
North American Industry Classification System (naics_sector)
Note: naics_sector indices and their respective industry:

Total = 00

Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting = 11

Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction = 21

Utilities = 22

Construction = 23

Manufacturing = 31-33

Wholesale Trade = 42

Retail Trade = 44-45

Transportation and Warehousing = 48-49

Information = 51

Finance and Insurance = 52

Real Estate Rental and Leasing = 53

Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services = 54

Management of Companies and Enterprises = 55

Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services = 56

Educational Services = 61

Health Care and Social Assistance = 62

Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation = 71

Accommodation and Food Services = 72

Other Services (except Public Administration) = 81

Public Administration = 92

NA = 99



Source: https://www.census.gov/naics/?58967?yearbck=2017


Codecs_panel_clean |>
  group_by(naics_sector) |>
  summarize(medianPay = median(payroll_per_emp)) |>
  arrange(desc(medianPay))

# A tibble: 1,910 × 2
   naics_sector medianPay
   <chr>            <dbl>
 1 522293         407473.
 2 711211         293208.
 3 5232           214697.
 4 52413          186906.
 5 524130         186906.
 6 5231           176339.
 7 52311          162067.
 8 523110         162067.
 9 52312          156349.
10 523120         156349.
# ℹ 1,900 more rows



Among industries in North America, Utilities report the highest median payroll per employee at $105.31. Accomodation and Food Services report the lowest at $18.67.

Year

Codecs_panel_clean |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarize(medianPay = median(payroll_per_emp)) |>
  arrange(desc(medianPay))

# A tibble: 5 × 2
   year medianPay
  <dbl>     <dbl>
1  2018    50848.
2  2019    50565.
3  2017    45386.
4  2020       NA 
5  2021       NA 



2021 reported the highest median payroll per employee at $58.71. 2017 reported the lowest at $47.78.

naics_sector and Year

Codecs_panel_clean |>
  group_by(naics_sector, year) |>
  summarize(medianPay = median(payroll_per_emp)) |>
  arrange(desc(medianPay))

# A tibble: 2,804 × 3
# Groups:   naics_sector [1,910]

   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>
 1 522293        2017   407473.
 2 711211        2017   293208.
 3 5232          2021   248386.
 4 5231          2021   203980.
 5 52413         2017   186906.
 6 524130        2017   186906.
 7 5231          2020   177422.
 8 5232          2020   177010.
 9 523           2021   166128.
10 52311         2017   162067.
# ℹ 2,794 more rows



Among industries in North America throughout a 5-year period, Utilities report the highest median payroll per employee at $111.38 during 2021. Accomodation and Food Services report the lowest at $16.18 during 2020.

title: Katherine M.
text:
Your exploratory data analysis of the team datasets go here.

Codelibrary(ggplot2)
library(forcats)
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(downlit)
library(xml2)

source(here::here("data", "data_cleaning.R"))



Code#summary(merged_panel) # general summary of the dataset


Data Cleaning

Codeunique(merged_panel$NAICS2017)

merged_panel <- merged_panel |> 
  mutate(
    Industry = fct_recode(as.factor(NAICS2017),
        "Total" = "00",
        "Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting" = "11",
        "Mining" = "21",
        "Construction" = "23",
        "Utilities" = "22",
        "Wholesale Trade" = "42",
        "Information" = "51",
        "Real Estate Rental and Leasing" = "53",
        "Finance and Insurance" = "52",
        "Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services" = "54",
        "Management of Companies and Enterprises" = "55",
        "Educational Services" = "61",
        "Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation" = "71",
        "Other Services (except Public Administration)" = "81",
        "Health Care and Social Assistance" = "62",
        "Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services" = "56",
        "Accommodation and Food Services" = "72",
        "NA" = "99"))


ai_barriers <- ai_barriers |> 
  mutate(
    Industry = fct_recode(as.factor(NAICS2017),
        "Total" = "00",
        "Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting" = "11",
        "Mining" = "21",
        "Construction" = "23",
        "Utilities" = "22",
        "Wholesale Trade" = "42",
        "Information" = "51",
        "Real Estate Rental and Leasing" = "53",
        "Finance and Insurance" = "52",
        "Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services" = "54",
        "Management of Companies and Enterprises" = "55",
        "Educational Services" = "61",
        "Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation" = "71",
        "Other Services (except Public Administration)" = "81",
        "Health Care and Social Assistance" = "62",
        "Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services" = "56",
        "Accommodation and Food Services" = "72",
        "NA" = "99"))


AI Usage Over Time by State

Code# looking at how general high AI usage has changed by state from 2017-2021
merged_panel |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = state, y = ai_firms_high_use)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~year) +
  labs(x = "Firms by State", y = "# of Firms with High Reported AI Usage",
       title = "AI Usage by State (2017-2021)")


AI Usage by Industry

Code# high AI usage by industry from 2017-2021
merged_panel |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Industry, y = ai_firms_high_use, fill = Industry)) +
  geom_col() + 
  facet_wrap(~year) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank()) +
  labs(x = "Firms by Industry", 
       y = "# of Firms with High Reported AI Usage",
       title = "AI Usage by Industry (2017-2021)")


AI Usage by Industry (Excluding Total)

Code# high AI usage by industry from 2017-2021
merged_panel |> 
  filter(!str_detect(NAICS2017, "Total")) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Industry, y = ai_firms_high_use, fill = Industry)) +
  geom_col() + 
  facet_wrap(~year) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank()) +
  labs(x = "Firms by Industry", 
       y = "# of Firms with High Reported AI Usage",
       title = "AI Usage by Industry (2017-2021)")


Barriers to AI Usage by Industry (Excluding Total)

Codeai_barriers |> 
  filter(!str_detect(Industry, "Total")) |> 
  filter(NAICS2017 != "00", NAICS2017 != "99") |> 
  mutate(n_firms = n_firms/100000) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = NAICS2017, y = n_firms, fill = barrier)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Industry", y = "Number of Firms by Industry (in hundred-thousands)",
       fill = "Perceived Barriers to AI Adoption",
       title = "Near Half of Firms Across Industries See AI as Unnecessary (2017)")


Barriers to AI Usage by State

Codeai_barriers |> 
  filter(!str_detect(Industry, "Total")) |> 
  filter(NAICS2017 != "00", NAICS2017 != "99") |> 
  mutate(n_firms = n_firms/100000) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = state, y = n_firms, fill = barrier)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "State", y = "Number of Firms by State (in hundred-thousands)",
       fill = "Perceived Barriers to AI Adoption",
       title = "Near Half of Firms Across States See AI as Unnecessary (2017)")

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text: Title: AI Usage For US Companies

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Short Description of the Project:
With AI usage soaring in recent years, our way of life has drastically changed. For this project, we will observe how this surge has impacted businesses across the US.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
2-3 General Research Questions:
1.  How has company AI usage changed over time?

2.  How does company AI usage vary by location, such as state or
    urban/rural status?

3.  How might company AI usage vary by internal factors, such as
    company size and ownership?

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Reasons/Inspirations Behind this Project:
Our group is composed of Economics majors, so we want to do a project that is related to our field. Additionally, we want to focus on recently developing news in order to make our project as relevant as possible, which is how we landed on AI usage as our topic.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
One Potential Dataset:
Annual Business Survey (ABS) Data 2017-2023: https://www.census.gov/data/developers/data-sets/abs.html

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Communication Plan:
We will communicate primarily through GChat, and each team member will respond as soon as possible, with more leeway given during holidays and weekends. We will set up meetings outside of class on a needs-based basis, with all members having allocated 11am-12pm on Fridays for such meetings. More meetings may be scheduled depending on the workload of a milestone.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Roles:

Primary Point of Contact: Katherine Manuel
Technical Lead: David Torres
Style Lead: Isauro Reyes

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Conflict Resolution SOP:
If a team member misses a deadline, the other team members will reach out to them and determine how to proceed (i.e. if they need extra help, if we as a team need to request an extension, etc.). The matter will only be elevated to the professor if absolutely necessary, which we do not anticipate. For evenly split decisions where the team doesn’t know how to proceed, we will flip a coin/decide randomly. However, we don’t expect this to be an issue since there are 3 of us.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Appendix
Note: we moved our data import and cleaning to new R files, for the convenience!

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Introduction
Source: THE DIFFERENCES IN HOW CNN, MSNBC & FOX COVER THE NEWS pudding.coo
In this document, we will be talking about the data story structure of the article “The differences in how CNN, MSNBC & Fox Cover News.” This is accessed through The Pudding’s website, which we believe is a competition where people can narrate with the data. The author of this article is Charlie Smart.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What Did They Do?

Clustering technique, but simple visualizations.

Visualization animations based on user scrolling bring the reader’s attention to different, important aspects of the visualizations and different subsets in the data. Readers can hover the cursor over individual points.

Building the visualization with the reader.

Each visualization starts off bare. With each paragraph of text to the side of the viz, the author adds more data to the visualization, allowing text and viz to complement each other.   

Search bars & filtering options for specific visualizations. 

After the visualizations have been explored fully, the author gives the user a search bar, in which the user can input their own topics/key words and see how they’re reflected in each plot.
Audiences can also filter data points/subsets of the data, allowing them to focus on aspects of the dataset that they are interested in. 

Interactive text. 

Certain words (ex. Month names, definitions) in the text allow audiences to hover their cursor over it, which either results in the respective portion of a graph being highlighted or visualizations of the definition being described to pop up on screen. This allows audiences to achieve a more complete understanding of what the author is talking about.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Implications & Methodology

What’s the purpose of these plots?

Following the visualizations, the author makes light of the scope of political polarization, warning the user to be aware of the news they are receiving and the agenda they may be pushing. This caution makes the visualizations even stronger, as they highlight this warning in an extremely effective way.

What was the author’s creation process?

At the end of the article, the author adds a section on Data and Methodology, allowing audiences better insight into data sources/what choices went into data collection, and other underlying data aspects that would otherwise be hidden in the visualizations.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
CONCLUSIONS

The audience can draw their own conclusions based on which words are used most by the big 3 news outlets: CNN, MSNBC, and FOX. This is informative as we can see which ones are biased to certain positions or ideas. This is precisely what the authors of this project try to do by selecting keywords the audience might be interested in or events happening during that time, such as the Russian war, hurricanes, Trump, Hillary, etc. 
Most importantly, we gathered more ideas on the possible direction our project can take. As our project is on the AI implementation in U.S. businesses, we probably won’t do it based on words, but rather in companies, highlighting the outliers but also those left behind. What industries are not catching up in the AI implementation? What companies are close to the 100% AI implementation? Is it possible to get 100% implementation?
